[19:31] because it's pretty short, sweet and easy.
[19:39] It's one of my favorite of all the medicines in the world. This is my favorite medicine,
[19:45] to be honest. It is really beneficial for lots of conditions. Why won't you do what I'm telling
[19:55] you to do? So if you've never taken it, I encourage you to try it for something.
[20:06] But we're going to talk about dosing it. Don't just try it to try it. There should be a reason,
[20:11] of course. But if you have a reason, it's definitely worth trialing for a few months to
[20:17] see what it does and how it makes you feel. But I'm a huge advocate for low-dose naltrexone.
[20:26] If you know who Isabella Wentz is, she wrote one of the first thyroid books almost 20 years ago now,
[20:36] 15 years ago. She has given a foreword in other books and on other programs where she claims that
[20:49] naltrexone is pretty much a miracle drug, and I tend to agree. So benefits and uses of naltrexone
[20:59] are many and varied. If you order and do any reading or research on your own
[21:07] regarding low-dose naltrexone, you'll run across the LDN Trust. They have a website with some
[21:12] blog articles and research gathered. But there are also three books that have been published,
[21:21] and they're just LDN. That's it. That's the title of the book is LDN.
[21:27] And so most of what we're going to talk about is from those books. I have all three of them. But
[21:33] if I've said this in conferences before, if rheumatologists would use low-dose naltrexone
[21:42] as the mainstay of their practice, the face of medicine would change forever.
[21:50] It really would. Rheumatologists treat the most difficult and the most challenging autoimmune
[21:56] diseases that we face for the most part. And if they started using low-dose naltrexone,
[22:04] their patients would get remarkably better. Probably 90 to 95 percent of them would have
[22:10] no more symptoms. And it's way less toxic than most of the drugs they actually do use.
[22:16] There have been multiple studies using veterans. Our veterans are an easy population to study
[22:22] because they're all adults. And most of them want to find something that works for whatever
[22:28] their element is. And since TBIs and post-traumatic stress disorder are rampant in our veteran
[22:36] population, that makes them really perfect candidates for the kinds of studies that
[22:41] the VA has done using low-dose naltrexone. And it's been remarkable to see the benefits.
[22:47] So like if you can name a disease, naltrexone is probably going to benefit yet.
[22:53] Low-dose naltrexone has been around since the 80s. And so it's an old, old drug. It is used
[23:03] mainstream if you're not familiar. Let's do a little bit of history right quick.
[23:06] If you're not familiar with naltrexone, its main uses are opioid addiction and alcohol abuse
[23:14] in high doses, usually 100 milligrams per dose, sometimes twice a day. So it depends on how severe
[23:23] of the problem. But in functional medicine, we use microscopic doses, very tiny doses.
[23:30] That's why it's LDN, low-dose naltrexone. We start with a tiny bit and we increase it over
[23:37] several weeks to avoid side effects. And we're going to talk about that in a minute.
[23:41] The biggest issue with trying to use low-dose naltrexone is it is a great anti-inflammatory
[23:51] agent, but it doesn't work as well and may require much higher doses than what the standard use
[24:03] is usually about 4.5 to 7 milligrams a day.
[24:09] But in the presence of continued grain intake, low-dose naltrexone is less effective. So if you
[24:16] think about eating grains during the day and that pushes inflammation up, and then we take
[24:23] low-dose naltrexone typically at night, and then it pushes inflammation down overnight,
[24:27] what's going to happen by morning when the LDN has now worn off? Inflammation rises again and
[24:34] people don't feel good. So I really encourage patients to be grain-free before we try to switch
[24:41] or to add low-dose naltrexone to their treatment plan. I don't really hold it ransom, so to speak,
[24:48] but I really encourage them to work on going grain-free because it's just an up and down
[24:54] cycle of inflammation, no inflammation, inflammation. And so until the grains are really gone,
[25:01] the medicine is not going to be very effective. And because it's a compounded drug,
[25:05] they're paying cash out of pocket and it gets pricey to keep spending 30, 40, 50, 60 dollars
[25:12] a month buying pills when the intake is making the need for that medicine even worse.
[25:22] So helping patients understand the need to be grain-free before they start the LDN
[25:28] is really important. I don't normally write PAs for medicine.
[25:35] On occasion, a patient has persuaded me to write a PA, but I normally don't. I try to prescribe
[25:41] the majority of stuff off of Walmart's old $4 list. I don't even know if that still exists
[25:46] anymore. That's how long it's been since I've practiced in mainstream circles. But I really
[25:56] encourage people to work on getting rid of grains so that the LDN can work better. But I don't do
[26:13] PAs. I try to use a local pharmacy. I partner with a local pharmacy in Papillion in Omaha,
[26:21] who has licenses in a few states. And so they're able to send LDN to a few of the places where
[26:30] I prescribe. If I need to send it to Florida, or Xsolutions doesn't ship to Florida, they're not
[26:37] licensed there. Then I tell the patient to find your own compound pharmacy and they just feel it
[26:43] like a normal prescription. RxSolutions builds my card. So I send the medicine. Once the patients
[26:53] pay me, I sign the script and the pharmacy feels it and mails it directly to the patient. I never
[26:58] touch it. I never see it. It goes directly to the patient. So it's a nice arrangement.
[27:03] I just don't sign the script till the patient has paid for it.
[27:07] But that's why I like working with local because they're more likely to do that. And then you
[27:12] don't have tons of people or organizations, pharmacies, you're trying to send orders to,
[27:19] and they all have your card. Like it's just one. If it's not, if it's not, I have two compound
[27:23] pharmacies I use. One's local and one's nationwide. And they're the only two places that have my card.
[27:30] Everybody else gets a prescription sent to them through the toolbox in Octomatra.
[27:36] There's a question that is in the chat.
[27:42] Jacqueline says, would you recommend LDN for someone having extreme anxiety? Absolutely.
[27:47] She's also hypothyroid, recently increased NP. We don't use the word normal, Jacqueline.
[27:54] Just a reminder, we need to see the numbers. I don't increase NP if the reverse T3 is above
[28:02] about 10 or 11 because there's no need. There's plenty of T4 on board when that is the case. So
[28:11] more than likely the anxiety got worse, I don't know, when you increased NP unless that reverse
[28:19] was really, really low. But you need the reverse T3 to help you guide the dose for T4.
[28:27] So if no anxiety meds are working, it's not an anxiety problem. She is responding to physical,
[28:35] chemical issues, not mental imbalance. I would definitely get labs, re-look at her labs from our
[28:44] lab video discussion perspective, take a look at the optimals that we're aiming for.
[28:51] But getting a gene test is great, but I don't think that's going to help. I think we're going
[28:58] to have to look at chemistry and figure out what chemistry is rocking the boat.
[29:04] Of course, gut health is crucial. So if all her labs are in optimal range, not normal,
[29:11] then I would work on the gut and on cortisol. Is she on progesterone? That's always my first go-to
[29:20] when we talk about anxiety. That's my first thing. I'm going to put you on progesterone
[29:28] right away. So what dose progesterone and what is her reverse T3?

[29:54] or consider adding some pregnant alone. Pregnant alone, remember, becomes progesterone and cortisol.
[30:01] So adding pregnant alone may benefit her adrenal function.
[30:08] So we may have more of an adrenal problem than a thyroid problem.

[30:23] I do. I don't have it available at the moment, but she was at the range where it needed to be
[30:31] MP thyroid needed increased. So then it must have been below range. Yes.
[30:36] It's just gotten really, really bad to the point that she's currently can't even work.
[30:48] She was a dental hygienist. Yeah. I think there's more to this than we understand.
[30:57] Send me a message like either on Facebook or text me. So let's connect and let's look at
[31:03] her labs really close and in depth and see what else we need to do to try to help her.
[31:10] Because if you don't have all the stuff we need right now, then
[31:14] that's not going to help. Yeah. I don't have it all pulled off. That's okay.
[31:18] No problem. But I would encourage you to consider either bumping progesterone up
[31:23] and or adding some pregnant alone right away to help with the anxiety and then find all
[31:29] our labs tomorrow at work or whatever and shoot them to me either in an email, a PM or a text.
[31:36] Okay. Thank you. We'll figure something out.
[31:44] So this is how I start low dose naltrexone. And this is based on Dr. Wentz's recommendation to start
[31:55] low dose naltrexone at 0.5 milligrams. Many regimens start at one or even one and a half.
[32:03] A few compound pharmacies have even developed their own titration pack.
[32:09] And so you can just send them a prescription that says LDN titration pack and it'll start them
[32:16] and increase it every so often. I don't like those unless they fit my protocol
[32:24] because Dr. Wentz has recommended that patients start with 0.5 and slowly increase
[32:34] because many of the patients that have autoimmune disorders, she's specifically talking about
[32:40] Hashimoto's. I'm talking more generically almost all autoimmune disorders can cause some conflict
[32:51] when you start low dose naltrexone too high. What's the chemistry of an naltrexone? Does anybody
[32:58] know? Does anybody know the pharmacokinetics, the action? What happens when we take naltrexone?
[33:06] Does anybody have a guess? I don't mean to put anybody on the spot, but I like it if you
[33:14] know stuff. Does anybody have a guess?
[33:24] What does naltrexone do? Remember, it works with opioid and alcohol abuse.
[33:34] So it acts on the receptors. What? Jenny, I'm sorry, I was gonna see if I could throw it out there.
[33:42] I don't know. I read the book, but it reduces those opioid receptors and it blocks the signals
[33:49] in your body. Exactly. Yeah, there you go. Yeah, you're exactly right. It works on the same exact
[33:56] receptor. So that was a dead giveaway clue, Tracy, when I said remember, it's working
[34:01] on those opioid receptors. The Mu and Kappa receptors that opioids impact when you take
[34:11] an opioid. What happens? How do you feel? How many of you have taken Norco for a dental procedure
[34:18] or post-op for pain? You took some oxycodone or morphine or whatever. What does it do to you?
[34:25] That loopy, weird feeling that happens is working. It's the drug is landing on the Mu
[34:36] and or Kappa receptors. And that is what's triggering that response. Sometimes carry
[34:43] Tracy, sometimes it is an oppositional kind of impact. And you do get that apathy or anger,
[34:51] like everybody responds exactly the same, but you get those weird kinds of feelings.
[34:57] And so that is why Dr. Wentz says, don't start people too high and don't go too fast bumping
[35:04] this up. Because if you do, those opioid receptors are going to be triggered
[35:12] overwhelmingly in some people and you will lose them. They will be like, nope, I tried it. I've
[35:20] been there, done that, not going down that road again, didn't like that drug. And so the lower
[35:24] you start, the less of those side effects, the less of those problems you are going to encounter.
[35:33] So I tend to start with 0.5 and work up every two weeks. So every two weeks, my dose changes.
[35:43] I normally send a prescription for about six weeks first. And so that's about 84 peels.
[35:48] If my count is right here, they take one pill every day for two weeks, then two peels every
[35:54] day for two weeks, then three peels every day for three weeks. And they norm or two weeks,
[35:59] and they normally take it at bedtime. Now, every once in a while, I think I have two patients
[36:07] that said, oh, it kept me up all night. Oh, and I moved it to morning. I'm like, okay,
[36:12] that's fine. Just take it in the morning. That's fine. Don't worry. Like if, if they have weird
[36:16] genes, many of us do, they may not be able to tolerate, tolerate a suppertime bedtime dose,
[36:23] but I normally dose it at suppertime or bedtime so that the drowsy feeling that
[36:30] sometimes comes with naltrexone helps them with sleep because most of these people are struggling
[36:35] to sleep. I'm checking the chat to make sure I haven't missed something.
[36:45] Okay, I think I'm good. Then patients get instructions in their treatment summary
[36:54] to let me know a couple weeks before they run out that they're almost out of their medicine
[37:00] because remember, I don't send their scripts till they pay me. And so when they are almost
[37:07] out of their medicine, they send me a note, connect with the office and say, I need my
[37:12] refill. We tell them how much it's going to cost. They pay me. We send the script.
[37:17] And so the second prescription gets them to their normal regular dose most times. So I send the next
[37:23] script at one and a half milligrams because that's exactly where we left off last time, right?
[37:29] They're at three caps. That's one and a half milligrams. Do the math. Okay. And so I start
[37:35] with two pills of that 1.5 milligram dose. So they're on now three milligrams at a time.
[37:45] And then we work our way up. If they have trouble doubling or bumping up, oftentimes we'll send an
[37:51] extra 10 or 15 days of a 0.5 or 1.0 milligram so that they have a little bit of a bridge
[38:02] to work their way up. If they report they're having issues, but most don't have trouble with this such
[38:08] low doses because we're working on this slow titration. And so this again gives them a little
[38:19] while. And then we are at maintenance dose. So it takes a couple months for sure, 10 or 12 weeks
[38:27] to get titrated up to a regular daily dose of LDN. And then they can be on that indefinitely.
[38:35] But that also means they need to be mostly grain free indefinitely.
[38:43] Long-term, I try to send the 4.5 to 5.5 or 6. And every time we visit and have an appointment,
[38:51] I always ask, how do you feel? Like, do you think your LDN is working? Do you think your
[38:57] anxiety is better? Your sleep is better? Your inflammation is better? Whatever piece of them
[39:03] we're trying the LDN for, I want to know how it's impacting that piece. I want to know what's
[39:09] happening. And if they're at six milligrams and they're like, well, I think things are better,
[39:15] but not as good as they should be. I'll be like, you want to try seven? You want to try seven
[39:20] and a half? Like, we're going to keep going up. The highest I've really ever dosed anybody
[39:25] at was about 17 or 18 milligrams a day until we found the dose that really, really did work for
[39:33] him. But he was a big muscular guy. And so I just think part of his dose needed to be for his
[39:41] habitus. So you can increase up to about 8 to 10 milligrams as needed or even higher if you
[39:47] think your clinical decision making justifies that. Use whatever you need. As far as checking
[39:55] inflammation markers, using your CRP, your acid, all of those markers, there's no point in checking
[40:01] them any sooner than eight or nine months. Like, just don't. It's very frustrating for you
[40:07] to look at a CRP that's 10.6 and then the next time 10.1. Like, it's not coming down. Yes,
[40:14] it's coming down, but that's not a lot. And that's frustrating for patients when patients
[40:19] think they're doing all the right things and they are working really hard and they are
[40:24] struggling through every day. And then we're going to draw a lab on them that doesn't show
[40:29] significant improvement. And then we have to convince them that we really probably shouldn't
[40:35] have drawn that. Like, it's hard. So I just don't. Just don't draw inflammation markers,
[40:40] but about once a year, give or take. If you have one that you're really, really worried about,
[40:46] like you have a CRP that's like 30 or 40, I've seen them really, really high,
[40:51] you could consider drawing them at four to six months just because you want to see it coming
[40:55] down. But you need to warn your patient before you ever really talk about how high it is,
[41:02] is that we're not likely to see much change. Like, I just want you to be aware,
[41:07] it's not something that moves real fast. And so even if it's only a few numbers difference,
[41:16] that's progress. Sometimes it's not any difference that soon. So just warn them beforehand so that
[41:23] they don't panic and freak out and think that all their work is in vain. It's working,
[41:28] but the body, many systems in the body, especially insulin and inflammation work similar to a snowball
[41:39] rolling downhill. You can't stop it once it gets going without a lot, a lot of work and effort.
[41:46] And so that's an analogy I use a lot. And so when, when I think we've done a lab too early,
[41:53] I just explained that this process is, is going to take a long time. Your body is trying to heal
[41:59] and do a lot of tissue repair. We're going to have to be patient. And we're going to check this again
[42:04] in about six or eight months. And we're going to start to see it moving as you continue to work
[42:09] to improve your health. We got a question over here. Does it affect them if they have an alcoholic
[42:19] beverage? Sometimes yes, because it does block once it lands on the Mu and Kappa receptors,
[42:31] those receptors won't be triggered. So they can have the alcohol, they just won't get a buzz.
[42:38] And yes, if they have surgery and need narcotics post-op, they will need to be off their LDN
[42:43] or not use narcotics. They can still use Tylenol. It's not an opioid. It doesn't. And so I have had
[42:50] patients choose to stay on their LDN versus using opioids for post-op pain. So everybody's
[42:57] different, but they should understand and know their options. So yes, carry teach and let the
[43:03] patients decide with you and your guidance. Most surgeons do want them off of it, off of most
[43:11] medicines and vitamins and supplements for anywhere from three to five days, sometimes
[43:17] a week before surgery. So if they have to be off of their LDN for surgery or post-op care,
[43:26] then they have to start hydration over. However, they might not have to start as low as they were.

[61:43] material here. Whoops. I'm just clicking all kinds of bad buttons tonight. Let me fix that.
[61:51] Breast cancer shows some really promising results with ivermectin. So does some of the gut cancer,
[62:03] colon cancer. So there is more and more data coming out. And you can find boatloads of it. There's
[62:09] some in the reference material here as well. This is how it works. We're not going to go
[62:13] into much of that because you all want to know what it's really doing. So one of the mainstream
[62:23] uses of ivermectin that's quite common is rosacea. Rosacea is often found to be a little bit of a
[62:33] mite issue. And so not the same thing as a parasite, but a mite is a type of parasite.
[62:40] And so a lot of cases rosacea responds really well to ivermectin. There is a very expensive topical
[62:50] cream that is used in mainstream medicine, but it's really pricey. If I had a patient try to get
[62:56] it, it was like $650 for a month. And I said, well, I said, how about I just prescribe you some PO
[63:05] ivermectin? And he's like, okay, that'll work. Because he was fixing to go to the farm supply
[63:09] store and get some paste and put it on his face. I'm like, no, let's work on this from a different
[63:14] direction. And so he has a prescription for PO ivermectin. And so that's something to think about.
[63:23] If mainstream can use it for rosacea, why can't we use it for other things that are also improved?
[63:31] So the normal rosacea treatment is doxytetracycline, much like your acne,
[63:37] your other facial skin problems. Sometimes we use the metronidazole. There are a boat load of options
[63:45] out there. The ivermectin cream, I couldn't remember the name of it, is Soelantra. That's
[63:51] what it's listed as brand-wise. And so it's out there, but it is pretty pricey, like I said.
[63:59] But all of these expensive medicines aren't really all that needed if you just put them on
[64:06] about three to five days of ivermectin. So in some of the studies that we have related to COVID,
[64:16] the virus cleared in less than five days in the ivermectin arm compared to placebo group,
[64:23] which was 11 and a half days for ivermectin and doxycycline. So even ivermectin by itself
[64:31] cleared faster than ivermectin and an antibiotic. So it definitely improves viral load.
[64:45] Here's some more information about COVID. We're not going to dig into that because that's not
[64:50] what we're here for tonight. But this might be interesting here. ivermectin has been used in
[64:59] several studies against various cancers, and it works through a variety of cytokines and
[65:07] key molecules in our immune system. And so some of the studies have connected ivermectin to ovarian
[65:15] cancer improvements for sure. So the highest doses have been 200, I think that's, I don't remember
[65:27] what the weird U is about micrograms. I was copying and pasting from the article. But 200
[65:36] micrograms is a high, high dose. I don't use that high of a dose usually.
[65:42] Some more information that may be handy and interesting. Breast cancer is also
[65:51] ivermectin is used in breast cancer, especially if it is of an inflammatory nature. So just some
[65:58] things to think about that are out there. As far as typical dosing for normal stuff like rosacea,
[66:09] any kind of viral thing that's going around, if the patient thinks they have parasites,
[66:15] the typical dosing that I use is 0.4 milligrams per kilogram. And so I send the patient's weight
[66:21] on the prescription to my compound pharmacy. And I let them do all the math, because every time I
[66:27] do it, I'm wrong. I'll calculate it out. And Patu will text me and say, that doesn't come out
[66:32] right. It's really this. I'll be like, okay, let's do this. I'm always wrong, no matter how I'm at it.
[66:38] I don't know why. I used to tell God when I was in art in school, and we did drug calculations,
[66:44] I was like, please don't let me ever have a job where I have to calculate drugs. And he laughs at
[66:49] me now, because I calculate drugs all the time. Ideally, we want to take ivermectin on an empty
[66:55] stomach. But if it upsets their stomach, then have them have it with a little bit of something,
[67:01] a little bit of food, not necessarily with a whole big meal. But peak plasmas reach two to
[67:07] four hours after their dose. And you really want to avoid curcumin or cyclosporine at the same time
[67:16] they're taking ivermectin, because they will slow down ivermectin metabolism. And it hangs
[67:23] around much too long. And it's less effective. It costs not a time. Our patient pay is about 150,
[67:36] 160 dollars for 30 days of it. But most people don't take 30 days of it, unless you're using
[67:42] it for a long haul COVID. And if you're using it for a long haul COVID, that's a little bit
[67:47] different in the first place. But I typically will write for 30 days, because the most common
[67:52] thing I write it for is rosacea. And so if they have rosacea, they're going to need it again and
[67:57] again. There's no reason for them to have to reach out to me and get an appointment or get a new
[68:02] prescription when I can write them a 30 day prescription for 150 bucks. And they can use
[68:08] three or four days of it and save the rest for another outbreak. So that is really handy for
[68:15] for your patients as a general rule. There are some side effects of ivermectin. There are side
[68:21] effects with every drug. I have patients all the time ask me or tell me, well, stuff over the
[68:26] counter doesn't have side effects. Well, yeah, read the label. Benadryl causes hypertension.
[68:33] Yeah, things have side effects. So everything comes with a side effect. So make sure patients
[68:39] are aware. The most common, of course, are dizziness, likeheadedness, just not feeling
[68:45] great, losing their appetite, which may be very beneficial in the first place,
[68:49] because we don't like feeding germs that might be in the gut. And so they can lose their appetite
[68:55] for three or four days while they're on medicine, as long as they're not vomiting and having
[69:00] severe nausea and not able to eat at all. Sometimes it will cause some stomach pain
[69:06] and bloating, some diarrhea and or constipation. Remember this germ, this medicine is battling
[69:12] a germ that often lives in the gut. And so those are common effects, not necessarily bad effects.
[69:21] So make sure your patient is aware. If you're using this for a parasite treatment
[69:26] of some sort or a gut infection, like our gastritis patient early in this, we don't know
[69:31] what she's got. But she might be a really good trial for some ivermectin. But she needs to know
[69:40] we're we're working on your gut. So if symptoms get worse for the first couple doses,
[69:45] that's to be expected. And so just make sure patients are aware of what you expect to happen
[69:50] when they take this. Tracy says she just read a whole Mackey's and Tippin protocols using
[70:06] ivermectin and the bend is all for cancer. Yes, absolutely. An amazing work.
[70:12] And great information there. What symptoms would make us think parasites. So that one's a hard one.
[70:18] So anal itching is most awfully often associated with pinworms. So if you remember little kids
[70:24] scratching their booties all the time, that's usually pinworms. And they typically get that from
[70:30] being dirty, they play out in the dirt, they dig in the fish pond, they like, you know,
[70:37] they're outside all the time, that pinworms are way less common today than they were when I was a
[70:42] little kid, because we played in the dirt outside, we made mudpods all the time. But kids don't do
[70:47] that as much anymore. So ask your parents if you have a kid that the mom thinks they have
[70:53] parasites. Ask them about anal itching, scratching their booty, that kind of thing. Because oftentimes
[71:01] those are pinworms. And so they can be treated pretty quickly and pretty easily. Most of the
[71:09] time adults that think they have parasites report diarrhea and or bloating. They have pretty
[71:15] significant butt symptoms when they have parasites. So watch for those.
[71:28] So yeah, for the rosacea patients, you typically only use the the cream when you have an outbreak
[71:34] or like if the outbreak starts today, you might use it for like seven or eight days
[71:41] until it's gone. And then a day or so like, but yeah, it's not every day forever. It's too
[71:48] expensive to do that. So you typically use the cream for the outbreaks. But that's why I like
[71:53] the peel because like for, you know, five days of ivermectin, you can get that for like 30 or
[72:00] 40 bucks. And it's gone, like, and it's gone for a while. Normally PO ivermectin is way more
[72:08] beneficial for rosacea patients than topical. Because think about it. The rosacea is coming
[72:14] up from the bottom of the skin. Where's that coming from? From cell proliferation and division.
[72:22] And so if rosacea is going to flare, it's going to flare from stress, from, you know, scratching
[72:31] my face, like moving the germs around, like, think of the function of the germ and how the body is
[72:39] impacted. And then take a PO med versus a topical med, and you're going to see different improvements.
[72:49] Methylene blue is probably my husband's favorite medicine. Most of y'all are aware he has severe
[72:58] CHF. He was told about eight, eight and a half years ago that he had about six months to live.
[73:03] He is still kicking. So in fact, he's, we've been working on building a part time house
[73:12] here on our kids property. And so he's been able to help do that. He couldn't have done that
[73:18] eight years ago when he was so sick. But he's been on methylene blue now for about two years.
[73:25] Because if I learned about it, and I'm like, we are going to try it. And so it makes a huge
[73:31] difference. He is much more able to function and oxygenate using methylene blue when he skips it
[73:37] or forgets it. It's definitely a bad day for him. So this is one of the first meds ever approved by
[73:47] the FDA. It goes back to the 1800s. It's so old. If any of you read books, or if any of you have time
[73:55] to read books, there is a book called the book woman of troublesome Creek, the book, I'm gonna
[74:09] put it in the chat book woman of troublesome Creek. It's a book about the blue people in Kentucky.
[74:24] They really were blue, because they have they had a hemoglobinopathy where their body just did not
[74:30] make hemoglobin and oxygenate. And so this whole book, there's two or three in the, that the
[74:37] author wrote about these people. It's sort of historical fiction. But it was really interesting.
[74:43] It was my first exposure to this hemoglobinopathy. I didn't know we had blue people. I didn't know
[74:49] that was a thing. But it really was a thing. And they were shunned and hated and treated poorly.
[74:57] It was absolutely terrible. And so most of those, those people groups are no longer in existence
[75:07] because they've now married, you know, outside their clan. And the hemoglobinopathy gene is,
[75:14] is disappearing, like it just doesn't, it's not prominent anymore. And so it's not showing up
[75:20] in many people. But methylene blue was the treatment, they didn't know how much to dose it.
[75:26] And so a lot of times they would wind up taking so much of the methylene blue over time,
[75:30] that they would get sick from the medicine, not knowing how to dose it or appropriate dosing. Plus,
[75:38] a lot of times back then, they used poorly sourced methylene blue that was laced with
[75:44] lead or arsenic or other heavy metals based on where they found the methylene blue,
[75:50] had it mixed or whatever. And so they didn't have good sources. But methylene blue works
[75:55] really well, moving energy around, it literally helps improve mitochondrial function.
[76:02] So if you have anyone that is having trouble with energy,
[76:11] consider using some methylene blue, Jacqueline says she had a blue paper, a blue people patient
[76:17] that came in to see her. And it was always startling. Yeah, because you think, oh,
[76:21] my God, that's blue. Yeah, I just, I had no idea we had blue people. It was interesting.
[76:29] Methylene blue can be used to optimize mitochondrial and ATP production. A lot of athletes will use
[76:34] methylene blue to optimize breathing and oxygenation to be able to have better
[76:41] energy and function when they're training and or competing. That I know of methylene blue is not
[76:48] disallowed or restricted in the athletic world, like some of the other things, some of the peptides
[76:55] are. So, or even testosterone, steroids, those kinds of things. So a lot of a lot of athletes will
[77:03] find sources of methylene blue. I am a huge advocate because it makes such a big difference
[77:09] in my husband's symptoms. It does reduce brain fog, it enhances cognitive clarity,
[77:15] improves general energy. It was used originally for malaria Lyme disease and some urinary tract
[77:22] infections and even sepsis way back in the day. People report sleeping better. Why? Because
[77:28] they're oxygenating better. If you're trying to sleep and you have any little bit of snoring or
[77:34] sleep apnea and you're not able to oxygenate well because of those problems, you get better
[77:41] oxygenation. When you start taking some methylene blue, voila, you're going to sleep better. And so
[77:46] that makes a little bit of logical sense. Many times when functional prescribers start talking
[77:53] about methylene blue, the first thing that comes up is, well, I don't do IVs. I don't want to do IVs
[78:00] in my clinic. Does it have to be IV? No, you don't have to do IVs in your clinic. We use a
[78:06] capsule form, a PO form of methylene blue. And it works quite well. It can be beneficial in COPD.
[78:19] Certainly can. You just have to titrate the dose very carefully. The most common dose with
[78:27] methylene blue PO is generally about five or 10 milligrams. So we started with five when John
[78:35] started. We did five for a week once a day. And then we did five BID using a morning and early
[78:43] afternoon dose to give him better energy through the day. Because we saw right away he would feel
[78:48] good for a few hours, but then it was gone out of a system by mid-afternoon and he was tired and
[78:55] sluggish and struggling to breathe again. So we bumped him up to five twice a day. And now he's
[79:02] at 10 twice a day. And that gets him most of the day. By this time of the day, he's wearing out
[79:08] and getting tired. That's normal. That's okay. It's evening. It's okay to get tired this time of day.
[79:13] And so we typically dose it 10 milligrams BID as a general rule. We have used 10 milligram capsules,
[79:23] 15 milligram capsules, and 20 milligram capsules. We like the 10 BID best. We get
[79:28] a better coverage with that, I think. It does go through the kidneys. So if you have a patient
[79:35] with renal dysfunction, you want to watch that, maybe reduce the dose or keep it lower. You can
[79:42] take it up to three or four times a day, but the half-life is about four or five hours,
[79:47] give or take. And so try to dose it for optimal benefit. It doesn't bother him
[79:56] to not have as much in his system overnight because he sleeps well most nights. He sits up to sleep,
[80:03] but he rests well. Oh, I'm glad they're making it over the counter. Carrie says there's a pharmacy
[80:18] in Kentucky making it over the counter. It's a buccal trochee. So again, I use my local compound
[80:26] pharmacy for methylene blue. I have two that make methylene blue locally, premier compounding pharmacy

[80:42] RX solutions. That's where we get it most of the time, but both places locally provide it.

[81:46] and so we spend about $100 to get his 90 days at a time. It's pricey for some,
[81:53] but I think it's well worth it, and that's way less expensive than a hospitalization.
[81:59] So the dose does vary a good bit based on your dose and or quantities, but we get two
[82:08] tins every day for about 100 bucks. So it works well for us. As far as cautions, people always
[82:17] want to know what's the problem. There's a few problems with methylene blue. One that's not on
[82:24] here is it includes a sulfur molecule. If you look up the molecular structure, which I highly
[82:31] encourage you all to start doing if you have sulfur allergy patients. Many drugs,
[82:38] Lasix, Spironolactone, Hydrochlorothiazide, like the boatloads of stuff come with sulfur molecules.
[82:47] And so if your patients report to you that they had a response to Bactrim or some other sulfur-based
[82:56] drugs, you need to know before you prescribe a medicine if there's a sulfur molecule on it or
[83:01] on it. And so I'm all the time I just pull it up on Google or chat GPT, show me this molecule,
[83:09] and I look for the S. If there's S, that's sulfur. I don't prescribe that to patients
[83:13] with sulfur allergies. Sulfur allergies in general are typically not severe enough to cause a problem
[83:27] with supplement use, but some of us can't tolerate any sulfur at all, and I'm one of them.
[83:34] So I had a severe anaphylactic reaction to Bactrim one time. My sister was also allergic
[83:42] to Bactrim. My mother was allergic to Bactrim. So we have some kind of genetic thing going on.
[83:48] There are four daughters, and my mother and three of us have known sulfur allergies
[83:54] in addition to mom. So that's pretty significant. And so when I found that out, I was like, oh,
[84:01] okay, well, I won't take that anymore. So time rocks on, and I start using various supplements,
[84:08] and I start having these weird symptoms. And so as it turned out, it's a much longer,
[84:13] complicated story. But as it turned out, when I started Googling molecule pictures of some of
[84:22] the supplements I was using, I found sulfur in a lot of them. NAC is one of them.
[84:28] Cysteine includes a sulfur molecule. So NAC products have sulfur molecules. If you have
[84:36] patients who are sulfur allergic, you want to be very careful putting them on bunches and bunches
[84:42] of supplements that might have sulfur in them. So I always try to look at their allergies when
[84:48] I'm making recommendations. But in some of my charts, I also have a dot phrase that says,
[84:57] if you're allergic to sulfur, you might have trouble taking these supplements. You might not.
[85:01] Not everybody has that severe of a connection with sulfur problems like me. But I've seen it
[85:10] in several patients. I probably have five or six patients that have the same kind of response.
[85:15] So when we all had COVID, I gave everybody their NAC, and that's how I found out.
[85:22] My palpitations and heart rate, I went up to like 150 or 60 laying in the bed resting. I was doing
[85:29] nothing. I didn't feel good. I had terrible fatigue from COVID. I felt miserable, but only
[85:36] from fatigue. I didn't have anything else. No sore throat, a mild headache, but mostly from
[85:41] fatigue, just laying there. Couldn't figure out what was going on. So I started searching and
[85:46] googling and reading and trying to figure out why I was so miserably sick. I was this close
[85:53] to going to the ER. That's how scared I was with my heart. And then when I figured out what it
[85:58] was, I'm like, oh, okay, well, I just won't be taking any more NAC. And so we found it in a
[86:02] few other things over time. So you want to be very cautious with methylene blue and sulfur
[86:08] allergies because it does contain a sulfur molecule. We don't give it to pregnant patients.
[86:15] We don't want to stain babies or anything weird like that. It will turn their urine bright blue,
[86:23] kind of blue green, teal green. So make sure they are aware that they can stain their toilet bowls,
[86:30] their clothing. If urine drips on clothing or if they're older male dribbling with
[86:37] BPH problems, they may can stain their clothes. So just make sure you're alerting them to those
[86:43] issues. But our big problems are with hemolytic anemia, those specifically that have the G6PD
[86:50] deficiency. So this is a problem with high doses of vitamin C over about 20 or 30,000 a day,
[86:58] or IV vitamin C that may be administered to a patient. Before you do doses that high of
[87:04] vitamin C, you are supposed to draw a G6PD lab test and see if they are deficient in that
[87:14] so that we don't hurt them. Then you want to be cautious using methylene blue with SSRIs or SNRIs
[87:23] because of serotonin syndrome likelihood. Methylene blue can contribute to some of the
[87:31] changes in brain chemistry resulting in higher serotonin. And if you give them
[87:37] SSRIs or SNRIs and we're raising serotonin that way, they can wind up with a sick
[87:45] serotonin syndrome big time. Again, these are my rules for working with compound pharmacy.
